title: Data

jokes.text: How did he write the note?
jokes.setup: The president's speech describes a little boy who asked Santa to teach him how to read for Christmas.
jokes.punchline: A staffer immediately punctures the heartwarming anecdote with brutal logical precision: if he can't read, how did he write the note?
jokes.explanation: A perfect Veep cold-open joke: ruthlessly economical (five words), devastatingly logical, and completely in character for a room full of political operatives who treat human interest stories as product. The joke earns its score because it requires zero setup beyond what was just said, the reversal is airtight, and it encapsulates the show's thesis about the cynical machinery behind political empathy. It lands like a slap.

jokes.text: Well, they do need to stay thin.
jokes.setup: The speech pivots to an HIV-positive girl who dreams of being a ballerina.
jokes.punchline: A staffer non-ironically endorses the thinness requirement of ballet in the context of a terminally ill child — an absolutely monstrous observation delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The joke's darkness comes from the complete absence of malice — the character genuinely doesn't register how awful the observation is. The straight-faced delivery in context makes it deeply uncomfortable and funny. Good economy, very on-brand for the show.

jokes.text: I preferred the Internet when it was just AltaVista and that little Star Wars kid.
jokes.setup: The team is panicking about Reddit users identifying the HIV girl from the president's speech.
jokes.punchline: Ben's nostalgic lament for a simpler, more benign internet — evoking two very specific early-internet touchstones — perfectly characterizes an out-of-touch senior political operative.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'AltaVista' and 'Star Wars kid' sells it — it's a precisely calibrated period reference that characterizes Ben as a man stuck in the early 2000s. The joke also works structurally because it ignores the actual crisis to focus on a nostalgic complaint.

jokes.text: Well, there's a town with no Gay Pride parade or a goddamn library.
jokes.setup: The team reports that parents in Pine Hill, Alabama are keeping kids home because they don't want them to 'catch AIDS.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's response isn't sympathy or outrage — it's a withering class/regional sneer that says more about her than the town.
jokes.explanation: Pure Selina: instead of engaging with the crisis, she pivots to coastal-elite contempt that reveals her own smallness. The two-item list (Gay Pride + library) is perfectly calibrated — two things that signal education and tolerance, delivered as a diagnosis. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Oh, thank you, Question Man. You just saved the entire city. Or did you?
jokes.setup: Someone asks the obvious questions: where did the data come from? Why didn't they get parental permission?
jokes.punchline: Ben's sarcastic mock-heroic response dismisses the legitimate question while also undercutting himself with the trailing 'Or did you?' — which is actually the correct answer.
jokes.explanation: Solid Veep-flavored sarcasm. The 'Or did you?' deflation is a nice touch — it's Ben half-admitting the question is valid while still being dismissive. Functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: This is catching fire like a gas station in a Michael Bay movie.
jokes.setup: The crisis is escalating rapidly.
jokes.punchline: Ben's simile is vivid, specific, and perfectly calibrated — Michael Bay movies are known for their ludicrously over-the-top explosions, making the gas station comparison both funny and apt.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed simile with the right level of pop-culture specificity. Not the sharpest line in the episode but earns its place in Ben's voice — he's the kind of guy who processes political catastrophe through blockbuster movie references.

jokes.text: Bozos, disassemble.
jokes.setup: The team needs to split up and get more information.
jokes.punchline: Ben's dismissal — deliberately echoing 'Autobots, roll out' but replacing 'Autobots' with 'Bozos' — is both a command and an insult delivered in one breath.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — two words do the work of a paragraph. The implicit Transformers reference (whether intentional or not) adds a layer, and 'Bozos' as the operative noun captures the entire team dynamic. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I always feel like the ruching on this one really accentuates your figure. Okay, you're right. Let's go with that.
jokes.setup: Immediately after the crisis meeting, we cut to Selina's dressing room where Gary is helping her choose an outfit.
jokes.punchline: The tonal whiplash — from 'child with HIV outed by the government' to 'ruching accentuates your figure' — is the joke. Selina's instant pivot to vanity is pure character.
jokes.explanation: The joke is structural — it's about the cut, not the line itself. The ruching comment is so mundane in context that it reads as a perfect encapsulation of Selina's narcissistic priority-ordering. Gary's sycophantic specificity ('ruching') is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Catherine, you are smoking? I'm vaping. That's not the point.
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Catherine about a photograph.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's correction ('I'm vaping') is technically accurate but reveals she's missed the entire point of her mother's concern — the optics, not the act. Her 'that's not the point' is equally oblivious.
jokes.explanation: A functional joke about the vaping-vs-smoking distinction that also shows Catherine's defensive obliviousness. Not particularly original — the 'technically correct but missing the point' beat is common — but it works in context.

jokes.text: He photographs well for his age.
jokes.setup: Selina observes that Catherine is still seeing Jason.
jokes.punchline: Instead of commenting on the relationship itself, Selina evaluates the boyfriend purely through the lens of optics/photography — which is both a political-brain comment and a deeply shallow one.
jokes.explanation: A compact, perfectly delivered Selina line. 'For his age' implies he's older (already a judgment) and the entire evaluation being photogenic rather than human is pitch-perfect character comedy. Could only come from Selina.

jokes.text: Is it a good apple? It's a baking apple. Fuck 'em.
jokes.setup: The Apple Growers Association wants to name a new apple variety after Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's one criterion for the honor — is it a good apple? — leads to the revelation it's a baking apple (inferior, secondary), and her instant, profane rejection is perfect.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. The comedic structure is immaculate: the setup (naming an apple after the president) is already absurd; Selina's question is exactly what we'd expect from her vanity; the answer ('baking apple') perfectly punctures the honor's prestige; and 'Fuck 'em' is the ideal two-word landing. Maximum economy — the entire joke runs three exchanges. Highly quotable, reveals character, and gets funnier on rewatch when you understand how seriously Selina takes her own status. Justifies the 8+ score by being a near-perfect miniature joke.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I look like Grandpa. What did they do to my nose? Mom, what about me? Huh? Yeah, I know. This is so disrespectful.
jokes.setup: Selina and Catherine look at a Photoshopped image in the Post showing what they'd look like after eight years in power.
jokes.punchline: Selina is entirely absorbed in her own aged appearance; Catherine tries to get a word in about how SHE looks; Selina brushes it off with 'Yeah, I know' — about her own face — before returning to 'This is so disrespectful' (also about herself).
jokes.explanation: A well-executed cringe beat where Selina's narcissism is so complete she can't even process that Catherine is asking about herself. The 'Huh?' is the tell — Selina genuinely didn't hear the question. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Yeah, I know, he's fluffing the hacks.
jokes.setup: A staffer tells Selina it's not a good time to barge into Mike's press gaggle.
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual dismissal — 'he's fluffing the hacks' — is a vivid and degrading description of a press secretary managing reporters, delivered with complete indifference.
jokes.explanation: A good Veep insult that works on the level of professional degradation — 'fluffing' carries its vulgar connotation, and 'hacks' as the object makes it a twofer. Economical and in Selina's voice.

jokes.text: Is Mike sharing his donut holes with y'all?
jokes.setup: Selina barges into the press gaggle mid-crisis, needing to manage the reporters.
jokes.punchline: She opens with a breezy, folksy non-question about donut holes — weaponized cheerfulness as political deflection. The contrast between the crisis backstage and her sunny performance is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the performance mode switch — Selina instantly snaps into affable politician. The donut holes are so specific and banal that the contrast with the HIV crisis lands as comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, of course, yeah. And also my name. Yes. It's Lyle. It's Lyle and it's my pleasure.
jokes.setup: Selina thanks a reporter named Lyle for coming to the Easter egg roll, saying she remembers his daughter.
jokes.punchline: The reporter pointedly notes she also remembered his name — a passive-aggressive dig that Selina misses entirely, cheerfully confirming 'It's Lyle and it's my pleasure.'
jokes.explanation: The running joke of Selina not remembering 'Lee/Lyle/Ellie/Chloe' is planted here — she clearly can't remember anyone's name but performs like she does. Lyle's quiet 'and also my name' is a good straight-man beat that earns its laugh.

jokes.text: I mean, you know, I'm the rock star. She's not the rock star.
jokes.setup: Selina is asking reporters to give Catherine privacy.
jokes.punchline: Her justification is that Catherine isn't famous — Selina is the famous one. The total unselfconsciousness of calling herself a 'rock star' while asking for her daughter's privacy is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The double use of 'rock star' (emphatic and then dismissive) is good economy. The joke reveals that Selina's concern for Catherine is partly about maintaining the hierarchy — Catherine is a supporting player in the Selina Meyer Show.

jokes.text: With respect, ma'am, do you think those same privacies should be given to an eight-year-old girl with HIV?
jokes.setup: Selina has just given a speech about giving her daughter privacy.
jokes.punchline: The reporter's question perfectly and publicly demolishes the entire premise of Selina's charm offensive — the 'with respect' making it even more pointed.
jokes.explanation: The perfect trap — Selina walked right into it. The structural elegance is that Selina set up her own punchline by making the privacy argument, and the reporter's question is both devastating and completely fair. The cringe comes from watching Selina squirm.

jokes.text: Where did HIV come from? I think some guy fucked a monkey.
jokes.setup: After the chaotic press gaggle where Selina blurted out 'HIV,' the team reconvenes and someone asks where HIV came from.
jokes.punchline: Mike (or a staffer) answers the question literally — the scientific origin theory for HIV — rather than addressing what HIV is doing in this press conference.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in misdirection. The question 'Where did HIV come from?' has an obvious intended meaning in context (why did Selina say it?), and the response answers the wrong question with complete confidence. The vulgarity is exactly right — blunt and scientific simultaneously. Highly quotable, lands hard, gets funnier on rewatch.

jokes.text: That's not just a breach. That's a data rupture.
jokes.setup: The team explains the data breach to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina tries to establish dominance over the terminology by upgrading 'breach' to 'rupture' — a distinction that means nothing but sounds more serious, revealing her management-by-rebranding instinct.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — Selina's instinct to reframe rather than address is perfectly captured in this one beat. 'Rupture' sounds worse, which isn't strategically useful, making it a perfect example of her instincts misfiring.

jokes.text: All of these words are meaningless to me.
jokes.setup: Ben talks about 'data fluidity' as an explanation for why the culprit is hard to find.
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts through the jargon fog with complete honesty — and the honesty is the joke, because she's the president and should probably understand this.
jokes.explanation: Good economy, solid character moment. Not the sharpest line in the episode but it earns its laugh — Selina's impatience with jargon combined with her complete ignorance is a reliable vein for the show.

jokes.text: Well, the press doesn't need the culprit. The press needs a culprit.
jokes.setup: Ben says the culprit will be hard to pin down.
jokes.punchline: Selina distinguishes between 'the culprit' (actual guilt) and 'a culprit' (a body to throw), with perfect rhetorical elegance.
jokes.explanation: The best single line in the episode and one of the stronger Veep lines in the series. The entire joke is the indefinite vs. definite article distinction ('the' vs. 'a') — a purely grammatical difference that encapsulates everything corrupt about political spin culture. Perfect economy (10 words), devastating wit, completely in Selina's voice, and functions as a political thesis statement. Earns the 8.6 craft score by being genuinely aphoristic — it's the kind of line that gets quoted outside the show.

jokes.text: You know, there are hordes of young women who roam the halls of the West Wing. 15% of them were hired to be fired. Yeah. We call them the Expendabelles.
jokes.setup: The team needs to find a scapegoat from the staff.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that there's a deliberate, named practice of hiring disposable women — 'Expendabelles' — is both horrifying and absurdly matter-of-fact.
jokes.explanation: The portmanteau 'Expendabelles' (Expendables + belles) is genuinely clever wordplay that does double duty — it references a macho action franchise while feminizing it, and the casual institutional nature of the practice ('we call them') makes it both funny and genuinely dark. Selina's response ('That's not funny, boys') is the correct response, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Just another Saturday night.
jokes.setup: Dan is assigned to go fire a young female staffer.
jokes.punchline: Amy's dry aside implies this is routine for Dan — firing/exploiting women is his regular weekend activity.
jokes.explanation: A solid dry one-liner from Amy that works on the level of character commentary — she knows Dan's social life and his professional behavior rhyme. Selina's 'That's not funny, Amy' response is the kicker, which adds another layer.

jokes.text: That's not funny, Amy. What? Why not?
jokes.setup: Selina tells Amy her 'Saturday night' crack isn't funny, right after Selina herself said 'sacrifice a virgin.'
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'What? Why not?' is perfect — she can't understand why hers is wrong when Selina's was fine.
jokes.explanation: The callback lands because Amy's confusion is completely justified — Selina's double standard is perfectly exposed. The exchange works as a beat within the running hypocrisy gag.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. And I'm in charge of the canning, Jonah. I'm the Dan who can.
jokes.setup: Dan says he's in charge of the firings.
jokes.punchline: 'The Dan who can' is a rhyming self-aggrandizement — a rhyme on 'the man who can' — delivered with Dan's characteristic oily confidence.
jokes.explanation: A fun little Dan-ism. The rhyme is obvious but the delivery and context (he's about to throw people under the bus) give it teeth. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: What about your side dick here? You tell him stuff? Oh, God, yes. He was more involved than I was. He was up to his thighs in it.
jokes.setup: Dan deflects Jonah's question by immediately throwing his aide under the bus to Jonah.
jokes.punchline: Jonah enthusiastically confirms the aide's involvement with escalating specificity ('up to his thighs in it'), not realizing he's helping Dan build a scapegoat.
jokes.explanation: 'Side dick' as a term for an aide is a vivid Jonah coinage, and 'up to his thighs in it' is a nicely grotesque image. The dynamic — Jonah unknowingly destroying his own assistant — is good situational comedy.

jokes.text: The Police. Law and order mixed with very mild reggae. It's like a comforting balance.
jokes.setup: The team is choosing walk-on music for VP Doyle.
jokes.punchline: Describing The Police as 'law and order mixed with very mild reggae' that creates 'a comforting balance' is a perfectly bureaucratic way to analyze a rock band.
jokes.explanation: A neatly observed joke — treating The Police like a policy proposal ('comforting balance') is exactly the kind of category error a political operative would make. Sets up the 'Every Breath You Take' callback beautifully.

jokes.text: Right now I've gotta go. This goat's not gonna scape itself.
jokes.setup: Dan wraps up his conversation with Jonah.
jokes.punchline: Dan's casual use of 'scapegoat' back-formed into a verb ('scape itself') is a confident, knowing wink — he's cheerfully announcing he's going to destroy someone.
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that also does character work — Dan is gleeful about this. The construction is inventive: deconstructing 'scapegoat' into a transitive verb is the kind of wordplay the Veep writers' room excels at.

jokes.text: I feel persecuted. I'm just waiting for the press to throw me in a pond to see if I'll float.
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about media coverage.
jokes.punchline: The witch trial metaphor — being thrown in a pond to see if you float — is both apt and revealing of Selina's martyr complex.
jokes.explanation: A solid extended metaphor that plays on Selina's sense of victimhood. The witch trial imagery is appropriate to a political scandal and slightly anachronistic in a way that reveals character. Works better in context than in isolation.

jokes.text: Honeymoon's over, isn't it? It was kind of a shitty honeymoon, too. Just like my actual honeymoon with Daddy. I know, Mom.
jokes.setup: Selina reflects on her presidency's early struggles.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from political metaphor to actual personal disclosure — her honeymoon with Daddy was also shitty — and Catherine's exhausted 'I know, Mom' reveals the depth of dysfunction.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on the extension of the metaphor into uncomfortable personal territory, with Catherine's weary 'I know, Mom' doing the comedic heavy lifting — it implies she's heard this before, which is worse than the disclosure itself.

jokes.text: 'The Onion' did a parody of that photo in the 'Post.' Oh, really? I like 'The Onion.' What you'll look like when you're leaving office, except you look exactly like you do now. Okay. They think you'll only be president for a few months. That's the joke. No, no, I get it, yeah. That's funny. That's funny stuff.
jokes.setup: Catherine tells Selina about an Onion parody of the aging photo.
jokes.punchline: Catherine explains the joke (Selina will look the same because she'll only last a few months) while Selina performs understanding she clearly doesn't have. 'That's funny. That's funny stuff.' is the tell.
jokes.explanation: The cringe comes from Catherine having to explain the joke to her mother, who then pretends she got it all along. 'That's funny. That's funny stuff.' is a perfect expression of someone performing laughter they don't feel. The recursive awkwardness (explaining an Onion joke that's about her) is good.

jokes.text: It's like my mom's cat. The president is just trying to help children.
jokes.setup: Someone says the crisis has gotten way too big.
jokes.punchline: Gary's comparison — it's like his mom's cat — is completely unexplained and apparently unrequested. The refusal to elaborate while pivoting to defending Selina is very Gary.
jokes.explanation: The unexplained cat simile is absurdist in a very Veep way — it's not random, it's the kind of thing someone like Gary would say when reaching for an analogy and failing. The pivot to 'just trying to help children' is a perfect Gary sycophantic non-sequitur.

jokes.text: Maybe because, Gary, we ruined a girl's life. Negative, negative, negative.
jokes.setup: Gary wonders why everyone focuses on the negative.
jokes.punchline: Mike's answer is the correct one ('we ruined a girl's life'), but Gary's response ('Negative, negative, negative') treats the moral reality as a problem of framing.
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'Negative, negative, negative' is the episode's purest expression of political spin culture — he's treating 'we ruined a girl's life' as a framing problem rather than a moral reality. The rhythm of the three-beat repetition is good comedy writing.

jokes.text: Great, now parents hate us. Oh, God, welcome to my childhood. And adulthood, actually.
jokes.setup: The National Parents Group has cut their support.
jokes.punchline: Selina pivots to personal grievance — parents have always hated her — and then extends it to adulthood, revealing how deep the wound goes.
jokes.explanation: The extension to 'adulthood, actually' is the kicker — she thought about it and decided to include all of it. A nice quick character beat that reveals Selina's psychological damage with minimal fuss.

jokes.text: If you see a fire, do you look at the fire and put it out or do you just say, 'I'm aware of it'?
jokes.setup: Sue challenges Mike on why the story hasn't been shut down yet.
jokes.punchline: Sue's fire metaphor is perfectly constructed and devastating — it exposes Mike's management style as pure inertia dressed up as awareness.
jokes.explanation: Sue's rare full-sentence takedown is the joke — she's usually so minimal. The fire metaphor is clear, brutal, and unanswerable. The follow-up beat ('Sue cannot tell me how to do my job. She just did.') completes it perfectly.

jokes.text: Sue cannot tell me how to do my job. She just did.
jokes.setup: Mike protests that Sue can't tell him how to do his job.
jokes.punchline: Ben's instant flat confirmation that she just did.
jokes.explanation: A perfect three-word Veep punchline. Ben's deadpan delivery of the obvious truth is a reliable comedic engine for the show. Not the most original beat but executed perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, I bet this baby could sure spill a lot of glitter, am I right? Put it down. That thing goes off in your face, the surgeon will be tweezering tiny shamrocks out of your eye jelly for a week.
jokes.setup: Jonah is at a party supply shop, apparently handling a large glitter cannon.
jokes.punchline: The supplier's response escalates from 'put it down' to a horrifically specific description of the medical aftermath — 'tweezering tiny shamrocks out of your eye jelly' — that is as funny as it is vivid.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'tiny shamrocks out of your eye jelly' is exceptional Veep writing — the shamrocks ground it in the Easter event context, 'eye jelly' is perfectly disgusting, and 'tweezering' as a verb is darkly precise. This is the kind of image that burns into your memory. Earns 8+ because the language is genuinely inventive and the comedy of the horrific-specific-detail is executed at a high level.

jokes.text: Grow up, huh? I do anyone. I did balloons for Jerry Ford in '76. Met him. Asshole.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to use the administration's prestige as leverage.
jokes.punchline: The supplier reveals he's done this for presidents before — and casually defames Gerald Ford as an asshole, stripping all political glamour from the moment.
jokes.explanation: The economy of 'Met him. Asshole.' as two complete sentences is great — each one lands separately. Gerald Ford as an asshole is a specific historical choice that works because Ford is blandly remembered, making the 'asshole' label a genuine surprise.

jokes.text: That's bottom of the range. You don't want that. I just named it Presidential because of that asshole Ford.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks about 'The Presidential' package.
jokes.punchline: The Presidential package is bottom of the range, deliberately named as an insult to Gerald Ford.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Ford grudge is perfectly deployed — the reveal that he named his worst package after a sitting president out of personal spite is both funny and character-defining for this minor one-scene character. The escalation from 'asshole' to 'I named my worst product after him' is a lovely comedic escalation.

jokes.text: Well, then you want the Star-Spangled Banger. Or Red, White, and Whoo-hoo.
jokes.setup: The team says they want to go all out.
jokes.punchline: The package names — 'Star-Spangled Banger' and 'Red, White, and Whoo-hoo' — are perfectly calibrated middlebrow patriotic kitsch.
jokes.explanation: Good naming comedy. 'Star-Spangled Banger' has a nice vulgarity hiding in plain sight; 'Red, White, and Whoo-hoo' captures the corporate patriotic kitsch register perfectly. Functional wordplay.

jokes.text: I don't give a shit, son. I still get paid.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks about creative control over the music.
jokes.punchline: The supplier doesn't care about any of this — his indifference is total and his logic is airtight.
jokes.explanation: A clean, functional punchline that closes the scene. The supplier's consistent contempt for political power is the running gag of this entire scene, and this is its final note.

jokes.text: Question is, how hot do you want them? Red hot. Gonna do the snail joke, right? That's your trademark joke. Signature joke. Yeah, you don't have to do the snail joke. It's so funny.
jokes.setup: Andrew is warming up the crowd for Selina's speech; she's preparing to go on.
jokes.punchline: The existence of a 'snail joke' as Selina's 'trademark' and 'signature joke' — implying she has so little material that one joke defines her — combined with Andrew's assurance that she doesn't have to do it (then 'it's so funny') suggests he actually really likes the terrible joke.
jokes.explanation: The 'snail joke' is a great piece of implied world-building — we never hear it, but its existence is funnier than the joke could ever be. Andrew's contradiction ('you don't have to' immediately followed by 'it's so funny') reveals his sycophancy.

jokes.text: No, no, no. You're gonna be fine. Look, the news moves so quickly, we'll have a new iPhone or the Chinese will be in Chicago.
jokes.setup: Selina worries that the HIV girl story is killing her politically.
jokes.punchline: Andrew's reassurance pivots from the mundane (new iPhone) to the geopolitical catastrophic (Chinese occupation of Chicago) as equally likely news cycle replacements — treating both as equivalent distractions.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'new iPhone' to 'Chinese will be in Chicago' is very funny — the paired options reveal a worldview where geopolitical catastrophe and consumer tech launches are equally mundane news cycle events. The specificity of Chicago (not 'America') is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Doyle's using 'Every Breath You Take' for his walk-on music. He loves that song. He got married to it both times. Every line ends with 'I'll be watching you.' Sting might as well be singing 'I'll access your medical data' in a fake Jamaican accent.
jokes.setup: The team is trying to change Doyle's walk-on music from 'Every Breath You Take' because of the data scandal.
jokes.punchline: Amy's analysis of the song — noting that every line ends with 'I'll be watching you,' then rewriting the lyric as 'I'll access your medical data' in 'a fake Jamaican accent' (Sting's affected Police-era vocal style) — is a brilliant piece of political/pop culture fusion.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's strongest sustained joke. The callback to the earlier Police discussion is perfectly earned. The rewrite of the lyric ('I'll access your medical data') is both funny and politically pointed. 'In a fake Jamaican accent' is the kicker — it's a real-world detail about Sting's affectation that makes the absurdist replacement even funnier. The fact that Doyle got married to it twice adds a layer of character comedy. Earns 8.3 craft because every element adds something and it works as a unified joke rather than a series of parts.

jokes.text: You having fun? Yes, ma'am. But also working hard. Thank you for asking. It's Chloe? It's Ellie. Lee. Lee? Lee. Ah.
jokes.setup: Selina makes small talk with a young staffer she's apparently met before.
jokes.punchline: The entire name exchange — Selina guessing wrong ('Chloe'), the staffer correcting ('It's Ellie'), Selina missing it again ('Lee'), the staffer correcting ('Lee'), Selina's satisfied 'Ah' — reveals that Selina can't remember this person's name, has apparently been told it before, and will never get it right.
jokes.explanation: The running name confusion gag is at its best here — the structure of the exchange (wrong, corrected, wrong again, corrected, fake satisfaction) is perfectly constructed. The fact that this is the staffer who will shortly be sacrificed adds dramatic irony on rewatch. Good cringe rhythm.

jokes.text: Fuck the light show, Jonah, okay? He's the vice president, not the new Lexus.
jokes.setup: The light show is synced to the music and can't easily be changed.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'not the new Lexus' comparison is a perfect deflation — comparing Doyle's entrance to a car commercial launch event.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economic line. The Lexus comparison reduces the VP's ceremonial entrance to product marketing, which is both funny and thematically resonant for a show about politics-as-performance. Specific enough to land.

jokes.text: Well, it's mostly self-help audio books and relaxation tapes. I'm quite an anxious man.
jokes.setup: Someone asks what he has on his phone to use as replacement music.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that his personal music library is entirely self-help and relaxation content — and his flat self-disclosure 'I'm quite an anxious man' — is a perfect minor character beat.
jokes.explanation: A well-deployed minor character reveal. The timing — in the middle of a crisis — is good, and 'I'm quite an anxious man' as a deadpan self-disclosure is a nice piece of economy.

jokes.text: Well, I'm not sure, but positivity is the first step. Love it.
jokes.setup: The AV tech says he's not sure he can change the music without screwing up the lights, then adds that 'positivity is the first step.'
jokes.punchline: Amy's sincere 'Love it' response treats a meaningless self-help platitude as an actual plan.
jokes.explanation: Functional comic beat. Amy's desperation makes her embrace empty optimism, which is mildly funny. Not the sharpest joke in the episode but earns its place.

jokes.text: 'Eye of the Tiger'? Any controversial lyrics in that?
jokes.setup: Someone suggests 'Eye of the Tiger' as replacement walk-on music.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's question — are there controversial lyrics in an instrumental-adjacent Rocky theme? — reveals how little he knows about music while showing he's at least learned to ask the right type of question.
jokes.explanation: Good Jonah character comedy — he's learned enough to ask a plausible question but applies it incorrectly. The gap between his attempt at professionalism and his actual knowledge is the joke.

jokes.text: What the fuck? Uh-oh. What? I don't know what's happened. You know, I usually use a Mac, so this is Windows 8's fault if anybody's.
jokes.setup: The attempt to change the music has gone wrong — 'Every Breath You Take' is apparently still playing instead of 'Eye of the Tiger'.
jokes.punchline: The AV tech's immediate absolution of himself by blaming Windows 8 is both a real-person tech excuse and a perfectly deflating moment of incompetence.
jokes.explanation: The Windows 8 joke is a nice contemporary specific — it captures both the OS's notorious bad reputation and the universal tendency to blame tools rather than users. Works well in context.

jokes.text: Screwed over by the Police. I'm Rodney fucking King.
jokes.setup: VP Doyle's walk-on music has gone wrong and 'Every Breath You Take' is playing during the data breach scandal.
jokes.punchline: Doyle conflates being 'screwed over by The Police' (the band) with being victimized by 'the police' (law enforcement), invoking Rodney King — a genuine victim of police brutality — as his self-comparison.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely inventive double-meaning joke that earns its high score. The wordplay (Police/police) is clean; the Rodney King reference is both shocking and logically connected; and the VP applying a civil rights atrocity metaphor to his embarrassing entrance music is perfect character satire. The economy is excellent — two sentences do enormous work. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor provided.

jokes.text: Twitter has it. ♪EveryLittleThing-SheDoeslsTragic.
jokes.setup: The music debacle has played out publicly.
jokes.punchline: The Twitter hashtag — combining 'Every Little Thing She Does Is Magic' (another Police song) with 'Is Tragic' — lands as the crowd/internet's perfect rewrite that encapsulates Selina's entire presidency.
jokes.explanation: The third and final beat of the Police music gag, and the best punchline of the sequence. The mashup hashtag is genuinely clever — it takes a Police song title, makes one-word substitution ('Magic' to 'Tragic'), and creates something that works both as a Twitter-era joke format and as a precise encapsulation of the Selina Meyer presidency. Earns 8.0 because it requires knowledge of two Police songs and delivers a thematic resonance beyond the joke itself.

jokes.text: They loved it, ma'am. You were like a rock star. Not Sting. Kurt Cobain. Well, not him.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to reassure Selina after the speech/music debacle.
jokes.punchline: He says 'rock star' then corrects away from Sting (directly implicated in the scandal), then corrects to Kurt Cobain — who died — then corrects away from him too, leaving nothing.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed three-beat correction joke. Gary's compulsive positivity leads him into a trap: he can't use Sting (too associated with the scandal), he pivots to Cobain (dead), realizes mid-sentence, and corrects again to nothing. The structure is perfect — each correction is forced by the one before it. The final 'Well, not him' landing in silence is a classic Veep beat. Earns 8.3 because of the structural elegance and perfect character integration — only Gary would construct this compliment this way.

jokes.text: It's like getting a valentine from your own mom, you know?
jokes.setup: Selina notes that her own supporters loved her speech — of course they did.
jokes.punchline: The valentine-from-mom metaphor perfectly captures the hollow validation of a partisan rally — affection that is guaranteed and therefore meaningless.
jokes.explanation: A sharp observational line that doubles as political commentary. The specificity of 'valentine from your own mom' (not just 'your mom' but the specific holiday card) makes it more vivid.

jokes.text: Ma'am, we're losing micro-donors. We'll be down to nano-donors.
jokes.setup: The data breach is causing donors to request removal from the database.
jokes.punchline: The scientific unit escalation from 'micro' to 'nano' — applied to donor sizes — is a nerdy joke that also captures the severity of the loss.
jokes.explanation: A clean little joke — the scientific unit ladder applied to political fundraising is a good category-mash that works quickly.

jokes.text: It's vital that these small-time nobodies realize we respect them, okay?
jokes.setup: Selina is talking about micro-donors who feel their data was breached.
jokes.punchline: Her summary of the constituents she needs to reassure — 'small-time nobodies' — perfectly contradicts the respect she claims to have for them.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly compressed Selina moment. The self-contradiction in one sentence — 'these small-time nobodies realize we respect them' — is textbook Veep character comedy. It requires no setup, delivers instantly, and reveals everything about how Selina actually views the electorate. Earns 8.0 because the irony is structurally perfect: the word 'respect' in the same clause as 'small-time nobodies' creates maximum contradiction with minimum words.

jokes.text: Do you think that's the best move, ma'am? The thing I just said that I should do? Do I think it's the thing I should do? Is that your question, Mike?
jokes.setup: Selina announces she'll make a statement on camera; Mike immediately questions it.
jokes.punchline: Selina's recursive repetition of Mike's question back to him — incredulous that he would question her own statement of intent — is a masterclass in Selina's management style.
jokes.explanation: The rhetorical repeat-back is a classic Selina technique — she uses it to humiliate people questioning her. The escalation from 'The thing I just said' to 'Is that your question, Mike?' is a slow build that lands well. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: If that happened, it would be hugely damaging and enormously problematic.
jokes.setup: Selina is mid-apology statement on camera when we cut to what appears to be a TV pundit/talking head commentary.
jokes.punchline: The cut mid-statement implies Selina's apology was interrupted or preempted by commentary predicting exactly what's happening — a meta-commentary on the coverage of the coverage.
jokes.explanation: A transitional joke that functions more as scene structure than pure comedy. The tonal cut works but the line itself is generic pundit-speak, which is partly the point.

jokes.text: Girl in the writers' room for the joint sessions speech. She was also at the rally tonight. Sci-fi Sally something. Ellie? Chloe. Marie. Claire. It's Lee. Gary is right, her name is Lee and she is a fine staffer. I see splashes of myself in her. I now regret that phrase.
jokes.setup: Dan pitches 'Sci-fi Sally' as the scapegoat; Selina and team try to identify who they're about to destroy.
jokes.punchline: Multiple people guess wrong names ('Ellie? Chloe. Marie. Claire.'); Gary finally gets it right; Selina says she sees 'splashes of herself' in Lee, then immediately regrets the phrase in the context of sacrificing her.
jokes.explanation: The name-confusion running gag reaches its darkest iteration here — nobody can remember the name of the person they're about to sacrifice. 'I now regret that phrase' is the standout line: Selina's self-awareness that 'splashes of myself' in the context of throwing someone under the bus is revealing, and she catches it in real time. Good escalation of the running gag.

jokes.text: No, fuck it, cheese. Oh. Uh, ma'am... Look, Kent, I'm sorry, but Chloe's got to go. It's not Chloe, it's Lee. And anyway, it's not Lee, it's Catherine.
jokes.setup: Selina is ordering snacks while simultaneously issuing firing orders.
jokes.punchline: The food order interruption ('No, fuck it, cheese') combined with the continuing name confusion ('Chloe...Lee...Catherine') creates a beautiful comic rhythm, with the final revelation that the real issue is Catherine, not Lee.
jokes.explanation: The food-ordering-while-firing beat is a classic Veep structure (mundane and momentous simultaneously). The name cascade (Chloe → Lee → Catherine) is the running gag's best escalation because the final name is actually correct and genuinely important.

jokes.text: What, is she nuts? I mean, it's gonna make it look like she thinks her mom bullied her.
jokes.setup: Catherine wants to work with an anti-bullying charity.
jokes.punchline: Selina's instinctive concern is purely about optics — not that Catherine was bullied, but that it will look like Selina bullied her. Which, as the audience knows, she did.
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony — the audience knows Selina IS the bully, and her reading the situation as a PR problem rather than a moral one is perfectly in character. The irony does the work.

jokes.text: Ma'am, cheese is on its way. Here's an interim banana.
jokes.setup: Selina demanded cheese; it's delayed.
jokes.punchline: Gary's solution — an 'interim banana' as a cheese placeholder — is delivered with total seriousness and perfect Gary logic.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's hidden gems. 'Interim banana' is an absurd bureaucratic construction applied to fruit — 'interim' is a political/governmental term applied to a snack, suggesting Gary has fully merged the political and personal worlds in service of Selina. The callback to the cheese order, the perfect deadpan delivery, and the sheer specificity of the phrase make it highly quotable. Earns 8.1 because the character integration is exceptional — this is pure Gary.

jokes.text: It doesn't even work. So do I leave tomorrow? You leave now. Unless you're on a tour, you will not be at the White House tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Dan has been sent to fire Lee.
jokes.punchline: Lee's question 'So do I leave tomorrow?' followed by Dan's 'You leave now' — and the businesslike clarification about tours — is a perfectly cold firing scene.
jokes.explanation: 'You leave now' is the right kind of cold Dan delivery. The tour exception is a nicely absurd bureaucratic clarification in the middle of a human moment.

jokes.text: What if I cried? Like the women do in movies? Wouldn't work. You're talking to a guy who once broke off an engagement at an Applebee's, then ordered dessert.
jokes.setup: Lee tries to bargain using emotional appeal.
jokes.punchline: Dan establishes his immunity to emotional manipulation with the perfectly specific anecdote: breaking off an engagement at an Applebee's and then ordering dessert. The Applebee's detail makes it.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke and one of the strongest character jokes in Veep's run. The Applebee's detail is genius — it's lowbrow enough to be funny on its own, but the 'then ordered dessert' is the precision punchline that makes it exceptional. Breaking off an engagement at Applebee's already suggests a certain emotional register; staying for dessert afterwards reveals a complete absence of conscience. The joke tells us everything about Dan in one anecdote. Comparable to the anchor examples: this is what an 8.5+ looks like — specific, character-defining, utterly quotable, and with a structure where each additional word adds rather than subtracts.

jokes.text: May I have your pass? It was a pleasure working with you... Lee.
jokes.setup: Dan fires Lee and takes her pass.
jokes.punchline: Dan remembers her name at the moment of her dismissal — the one time he gets it right is right before she leaves forever.
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the name-confusion running gag. The pause before 'Lee' — implying Dan either finally looked at the badge or made a genuine effort — combined with the fact that it's the last thing he'll ever say to her, lands as both a gag and a moment of genuine dark comedy. The running gag gets a worthy payoff.

jokes.text: Fitting that for Easter this story refuses to fucking die.
jokes.setup: The scandal is spreading despite the scapegoat.
jokes.punchline: Ben draws an Easter/resurrection parallel — the story won't die, like Christ — with casual profanity.
jokes.explanation: A smart, compact joke. 'Refuses to fucking die' is made funnier by the Easter context — the blasphemy is accidental and structural rather than gratuitous. Good economy.

jokes.text: Is there no other news? Whatever happened to Ebola? I loved Ebola.
jokes.setup: The scandal continues to dominate coverage.
jokes.punchline: Selina expresses nostalgia for a deadly epidemic as a preferable news story. 'I loved Ebola' is the kicker — expressing affection for a disease outbreak.
jokes.explanation: Three words — 'I loved Ebola' — do extraordinary work. The shock of a sitting president expressing affection for a deadly epidemic (because it was better press than her current scandal) is both darkly funny and a precise political satire. It's Selina's most revealing line in the episode: her hierarchy of concerns is scandal coverage, not human life. Earns 8.3 because the economy is exceptional and the character integration is absolute — only Selina would say this, and only in this context.

jokes.text: So we threw someone off the life raft. Did no one think to tell the sharks?
jokes.setup: The scapegoat firing didn't stop the press from digging.
jokes.punchline: Ben's shark metaphor is darkly apt — firing Lee was meant to satisfy the press (the 'sharks'), but nobody thought to tell them.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed metaphor that advances the plot while being funny. The logic is sound (you do tell the sharks) and the fact that they failed to do so is the absurd political reality.

jokes.text: Yes, ma'am. I've been fattening him up for just this occasion. I got him a staff. And I got him involved in Families First. I even got him on the campaign. He's the one who did the fireworks and the music for last night. That was him? Off with his fat head.
jokes.setup: Dan reveals he's been setting Jonah up as a scapegoat from the beginning.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Dan has been systematically fattening Jonah for slaughter — 'I've been fattening him up for just this occasion' — followed by Selina's delight ('Off with his fat head') is a sustained dark comedy beat.
jokes.explanation: The reveal recontextualizes the entire Jonah subplot — what looked like Dan throwing Jonah occasional bones was actually systematic preparation for sacrifice. 'Fattening him up' is a vivid and specific metaphor; 'Off with his fat head' from Selina is the perfect enthusiastic endorsement. Good callback payoff.

jokes.text: You know, that kind of surprised masturbator face.
jokes.setup: Dan describes Jonah's 'inherently guilty look.'
jokes.punchline: 'Surprised masturbator face' as a precise descriptor for Jonah's permanent expression of caught-out guilt.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely inventive insult. 'Surprised masturbator face' is specific, visual, and — if you know the actor's face — completely accurate as a description of Jonah's look. The construction 'surprised [X] face' is a classic insult template, but the choice of 'masturbator' is unexpected and precise. The 'Yeah, he does' confirmation from Ben is the perfect beat. Earns 8.1 for originality and character specificity.

jokes.text: Do it. Get rid of him. Throw him under a bus. If you can find one that's long enough.
jokes.setup: Selina endorses throwing Jonah under the bus.
jokes.punchline: The physical qualification — 'if you can find one that's long enough' — implies Jonah is so tall that a normal bus wouldn't cover him.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed capper. The idiom extension — taking 'throw under the bus' literally and then adding the physical constraint — is a reliable Veep technique executed cleanly. Jonah's height is character-specific, so it works as character comedy too.

jokes.text: Hey, Egan, VP wants to see the P. He thinks a bigger head needs to roll, okay? Like a massive Easter Island-sized head. Hmm. No. No. Look at me, I've got a small head. I could wear a child's hat.
jokes.setup: Jonah delivers the VP's message that a bigger head needs to roll.
jokes.punchline: Dan's defense against Jonah's implied accusation is not 'I'm innocent' but 'my head is small' — demonstrated by the claim he could wear a child's hat. The physical self-assessment as political defense is absurd.
jokes.explanation: The Easter Island head escalation from Jonah, then Dan's literal deflection about head size, is a good visual comedy beat even without seeing it. 'I could wear a child's hat' is a good specific absurdist detail.

jokes.text: Besides, I know about you spreading the Danny Chung torture rumor. Well, that's useless leverage right now, Jonah, because you have all the credibility of someone who hacked the medical records of a sick child.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to use his knowledge of Dan's past actions as leverage.
jokes.punchline: Dan points out that Jonah's credibility is currently so destroyed that his knowledge is worthless — a perfectly logical counterargument that also happens to be devastatingly accurate.
jokes.explanation: A smart political logic joke — Dan's counter isn't denial, it's a precise assessment of the leverage's current market value. The phrasing 'all the credibility of someone who hacked the medical records of a sick child' is both funny and logically exact.

jokes.text: Hey, 'I Am Groot,' stop swapping spit here with 'Pretty Woman' and get me Brock and Hunter.
jokes.setup: Jonah and his aide are talking to each other while Dan needs them to do something.
jokes.punchline: Dan dismisses Jonah as 'I Am Groot' (a character who can only say one phrase, implying Jonah has limited range) and the aide as 'Pretty Woman' (a Cinderella-ish prostitute metaphor for the aide's position).
jokes.explanation: Good insult construction. 'I Am Groot' as applied to Jonah is apt — Jonah's contributions to conversations are often repetitive and loud. 'Pretty Woman' for the aide implies he's been saved/elevated by proximity to power. Both references work as character descriptions.

jokes.text: Damn, man, this guy's balls are so big, they're practically tits. Huh? Johnny Tit-balls. Yeah, don't squeeze the milkers too much. Milkers. Love it.
jokes.setup: The party supply man is apparently handling/squeezing a balloon animal in a way that calls to mind anatomy.
jokes.punchline: The balloon guy's running commentary on ball-shaped objects turns into an extended sexual/anatomical riff that Dan finds charming — 'Milkers. Love it.' — while Jonah watches.
jokes.explanation: The lowbrow balloon humor is the weakest extended beat in the episode. 'Johnny Tit-balls' and 'milkers' are functional crude comedy but lack the precision that makes Veep's vulgarity work. The joke exists mainly to set up the sexual harassment beat that follows.

jokes.text: You were firing me? What the hell was that? Nothing. It's just jock stuff. You know, just guys having fun. That isn't, Jonah. That's sexual harassment.
jokes.setup: Jonah's aide witnesses the ball-squeezing balloon behavior and challenges Jonah on whether that constitutes the kind of workplace behavior he's being fired for.
jokes.punchline: The aide's flat labeling of the scene as 'sexual harassment' (which it technically is) against Jonah's 'jock stuff' defense is the joke — and it's pointed.
jokes.explanation: The setup (Jonah is being fired, ostensibly for data issues, but also for this kind of behavior) makes the aide's diagnosis sharper. 'Jock stuff' vs. 'sexual harassment' as contested terms is a good contemporary joke that lands with precision.

jokes.text: Jesus, does he do that to you all the time? No, not all the time. Sometimes, but not all the time. It's just high spirits.
jokes.setup: Dan asks if Jonah subjects his aide to this behavior regularly.
jokes.punchline: The aide's qualified answer — 'No, not all the time. Sometimes, but not all the time' — is both technically a denial and a confirmation, followed by his own (Jonah-esque) minimization 'just high spirits.'
jokes.explanation: The three-beat construction ('No, not all the time. Sometimes, but not all the time.') is a good cringe rhythm — each beat undermines the previous one. The aide's adoption of Jonah's defensive language ('just high spirits') after calling it harassment is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Mr. Ryan, so they were out of tuna melts, so I improvised and I got you this lobster curry roll. All right, look, zip it, Jeeves. I'm in the middle of firing your boss.
jokes.setup: Richard appears with a food order at the worst possible moment.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from tuna melt to 'lobster curry roll' as an improvised equivalent, combined with Jonah's 'zip it, Jeeves' during what he thinks is a firing conversation, is beautifully absurd.
jokes.explanation: The lobster curry roll as a tuna melt substitute is a perfect Richard non-sequitur — he's so literal and so earnest that the category error feels logical to him. 'Zip it, Jeeves' from Jonah is also good — calling his aide 'Jeeves' (an aristocratic butler name) while in a closet ordering party supplies is nicely absurd.

jokes.text: Wow, over the campaign data thing? The I Care mailer. All right, no? I'm picking up from your facial cues that you don't know. Here's four verbal cues for you... 'Tell me now, asshole.'
jokes.setup: Richard realizes Jonah doesn't know about a specific additional data breach — the I Care mailer.
jokes.punchline: Richard's 'I'm picking up from your facial cues' is self-aware comedy, followed by Jonah's perfectly calibrated threat disguised as 'verbal cues.'
jokes.explanation: Richard's 'facial cues' awareness is the setup; Jonah's mirroring of the language ('verbal cues') while delivering a blunt threat is the punchline. The structural parallel — Richard using sophisticated observational language, Jonah immediately weaponizing the same vocabulary — is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Okay, Yogi and Boo-Boo, you say nothing to anybody. You understand me? Yes. Well, obviously you don't, 'cause you just fucking spoke. Oh.
jokes.setup: Jonah gives a command for silence.
jokes.punchline: Richard immediately answers the rhetorical question 'You understand me?' — and Jonah calls him out for speaking, which Richard accepts with a defeated 'Oh.'
jokes.explanation: A clean comedy logic joke — the literalism of answering 'you understand me?' with 'Yes' is immediately undercut. Richard's 'Oh' acceptance is perfect. The Yogi and Boo-Boo reference (Jonah is tall/Yogi, Richard is short/Boo-Boo) is a nice detail.

jokes.text: Amy, the gates of hell have opened and you are my plus one.
jokes.setup: Dan discovers the additional data breach (the I Care mailer) and calls Amy in panic.
jokes.punchline: Dan reframes catastrophe as a social engagement — 'you are my plus one' — treating apocalyptic crisis as an exclusive invitation.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed panic line. 'The gates of hell have opened' is hyperbolic but earned; the pivot to 'you are my plus one' reframes hell as a social event, which is both funny and reveals Dan's character — even in crisis, he frames things competitively/socially. Highly quotable. Earns 8.2 for the originality of the framing and the economy of execution.

jokes.text: You cannot be associated with bullying because people are going to think that you were bullied by me. I wasn't bullied by you, I was bullied because of you.
jokes.setup: Selina is telling Catherine she can't support an anti-bullying charity.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's correction — 'I wasn't bullied by you, I was bullied because of you' — is a precise and devastating distinction that Selina cannot process.
jokes.explanation: Possibly the best character line Catherine gets in the series. The preposition swap ('by' vs. 'because of') is a surgical distinction that is simultaneously a joke and a devastating psychological truth — Selina didn't bully Catherine directly, she created the conditions for Catherine to be bullied by others. Perfect economy, perfect structure, completely in both characters' voices. Earns 8.7 for the precision of the language and the depth of the character revelation packed into one clause change.

jokes.text: Do you want to go to Hawaii at Thanksgiving? Are you interested in Memaw's sapphire earrings? Are you bullying me into dropping an anti-bullying charity?
jokes.setup: Selina tries to bribe/pressure Catherine into dropping the anti-bullying charity.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's meta-observation — 'Are you bullying me into dropping an anti-bullying charity?' — names the perfect recursive absurdity of the situation.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best constructed joke alongside the Applebee's line. The escalation from Hawaii to sapphire earrings to Catherine's devastating question is perfectly structured. The recursive irony — a sitting president bullying her daughter into dropping an anti-bullying charity — is the show's thesis statement made personal. Earns 8.7 because it's the most elegant encapsulation of the Selina-Catherine dynamic in the episode, requires no explanation to be funny, and the question 'Are you bullying me into dropping an anti-bullying charity?' is close to a perfect joke sentence.

jokes.text: Gary, am I? You're parenting. Yeah, well, too, I might add.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Gary if she's bullying Catherine.
jokes.punchline: Gary offers 'parenting' as the euphemism; Selina accepts it and adds 'too' — implying she's parenting extraordinarily well.
jokes.explanation: Gary's role as reality-adjuster and euphemism-provider is at its purest here. 'You're parenting' is exactly the reframe Selina needs, and her instant adoption of it ('Yeah, well, too') is perfect. The 'too' suggesting excellence is Gary-logic fully internalized.

jokes.text: Whoa, whoa, whoa, guys. / We'll explain later. / No, we won't. We'll never explain.
jokes.setup: Ben and Amy burst into Selina's meeting. Someone asks them to explain.
jokes.punchline: Amy says 'We'll explain later.' Ben immediately overrides: 'No, we won't. We'll never explain.' The nihilistic certainty of 'We'll never explain' is a perfect piece of political realism played as comedy.
jokes.explanation: 'We'll never explain' is a wonderful piece of political nihilism delivered as a matter of fact. Amy's automatic 'explain later' politeness is immediately killed by Ben's realism. The economy is excellent (seven words for two characters' relationship with transparency captured completely). Quotable.

jokes.text: Okay, Catherine, up, out. For fuck's sake. / All right, that's enough with the F words. Thank you very much. I apologize for her behav...
jokes.setup: Mike tells Catherine to leave. Selina starts to apologize for her behavior — then stops.
jokes.punchline: Mike presumably realizes mid-sentence that he is apologizing to foreign dignitaries for Selina's behavior — then doesn't finish, either catching himself or being interrupted. The incomplete sentence is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Mike beginning to apologize for Selina's behavior to the dignitaries she just was meeting — the same Selina who just told him off — is a good ironic beat. The cut-off sentence amplifies the awkwardness.

jokes.text: Well, then, why don't you tell me and I'll decide whether or not I know it?
jokes.setup: Ben and Amy say they can't tell Selina about the additional data breach because if she knows, she'll be implicated.
jokes.punchline: Selina's solution — tell her and she'll decide whether to 'know it' — is the perfect political-brain response: she believes plausible deniability is something she can consciously choose after the fact.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top three jokes and arguably the purest Veep joke of the episode. The logic is completely broken — you cannot retroactively decide whether you know something — and yet within the world of political deniability, it's exactly the logic that operates. Selina's delivery of this as if it's a reasonable compromise is the core character comedy of the entire show. Economy is near-perfect; character integration is absolute; the structure (setup concern → Selina's solution → the broken logic of the solution) is immaculate. Should be measured against the anchor 9.5 example — it's in that territory.

jokes.text: We're fucked? Is that what this is? 'I'm afraid it's spread to the liver' fucked?
jokes.setup: The team confirms that the broader data breach constitutes a federal crime.
jokes.punchline: Selina's gradation of 'fucked' — specifically invoking the medical metaphor of 'spread to the liver' — creates a vivid scale of catastrophe.
jokes.explanation: A memorable line that invents a specific sub-category of being 'fucked.' The cancer diagnosis framing ('spread to the liver') is both darkly funny and contextually appropriate given the HIV scandal. The construction — establishing a gradient of fucked — is a Veep specialty executed well here.

jokes.text: Don't give me that bunker shit. Hitler went into a bunker. When he came out, he wasn't chancellor anymore, was he? Plus he was dead.
jokes.setup: Ben suggests a 'bunker mentality' for dealing with the crisis.
jokes.punchline: Selina's rebuttal uses Hitler in the bunker as evidence against the bunker strategy — noting he 'wasn't chancellor anymore' (true, because he died) — and adds 'Plus he was dead' as a second, more obvious point.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant piece of political analogy comedy. 'He wasn't chancellor anymore, was he?' is technically correct but spectacularly misses the point of what happened in the bunker; 'Plus he was dead' is the perfectly deadpan secondary observation, as if it's an afterthought rather than the main point. The joke works because the logic is both wrong (he lost the chancellorship for a specific reason) and right (bunkers are indeed where careers end). Earns 8.3 — comparable to the 8.0 anchor level.

jokes.text: I'll tell you something, if he were alive right now, he'd be very anxious to distance himself from me at this moment.
jokes.setup: Selina has invoked Hitler as a cautionary tale.
jokes.punchline: She extends the Hitler comparison to suggest that if Hitler were alive, even HE would want distance from her current political situation — inverting the usual dynamic of 'I wouldn't want to be associated with Hitler.'
jokes.explanation: A perfectly inverted joke: the usual statement is 'I wouldn't want to be associated with Hitler,' but here Hitler would be the one avoiding Selina. The logic inverts the moral hierarchy — Selina is somehow in worse political shape than the most notorious figure in history. The delivery of 'very anxious to distance himself from me at this moment' is the kind of calibrated comic understatement that Veep does best. Earns 8.3 for originality and structural elegance.

jokes.text: A face. Although, if you operate last in, first out, I'm screwed.
jokes.setup: The team agrees that a major resignation is needed.
jokes.punchline: Selina applies an employment accounting principle (LIFO — last in, first out) to political sacrifice and realizes it points to her, as she's the most recently arrived president.
jokes.explanation: The application of LIFO inventory accounting to political sacrifice is a nice conceptual joke — it's exactly the kind of logic a political operative would use, and it's immediately self-undermining. Works well in context.

jokes.text: Yes, Madam President. This is a head-on collision and I'm your biggest air bag.
jokes.setup: Ben offers himself as the one to resign.
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'biggest air bag' metaphor is both a noble self-description and a physical one — airbags are large and soft, which works as a description of Ben, who is indeed large and whose whole career has been absorbing political impact.
jokes.explanation: A metaphor that works on two levels: protective function (airbags save lives in crashes) and physical appearance (Ben is the 'biggest' airbag). The double meaning is subtle enough to be clever rather than obvious. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Okay, so we need to issue a positive statement about the HIV girl. Not positive. Wrong word.
jokes.setup: Selina wants to issue a statement about Jennifer Graham.
jokes.punchline: Someone immediately catches that 'positive statement' is the wrong word given Jennifer Graham is HIV positive — 'positive' carries a double meaning that no one wants to invoke.
jokes.explanation: A good quick wordplay joke that also functions as a plot beat. The 'positive' double meaning is the kind of linguistic trap a good political comms team would catch. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: Mike, did I see you smiling when it was suggested I might be fired? Me? No. I... I wasn't smiling. I was stretching my lips because I have very dry lips, and my doctor said, 'You should probably stretch them.' So I was following medical advice. Like... Like that. That's what you're going with?
jokes.setup: Selina notices Mike's expression during the discussion of Ben's potential firing.
jokes.punchline: Mike's increasingly elaborate and transparent lie — 'stretching my lips' on doctor's orders — combined with his demonstration of the 'lip stretching,' is a perfect cringe beat. Ben's 'That's what you're going with?' is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best extended beats. The escalation from 'I wasn't smiling' to the elaborate medical justification is perfectly constructed. Mike's demonstration (physically showing the 'lip stretch' he was supposedly doing) is inferred from context and would be visually hilarious. Ben's 'That's what you're going with?' is the ideal dry skeptical response. The joke also reveals Mike's fundamental incompetence at the core function of his job — managing information. Earns 8.2.

jokes.text: Wow. Listen, you ever play poker, I want in, 'cause you are one terrible liar. And that's sad 'cause that's your whole job.
jokes.setup: Selina responds to Mike's lip-stretching excuse.
jokes.punchline: Selina's invitation to play poker (because she wants easy money) combined with the observation that Mike's lying is so bad it's professionally tragic.
jokes.explanation: The poker observation is good; the landing line — 'that's sad 'cause that's your whole job' — is what elevates it. It's both a job description and a professional eulogy. Earns 8.0 for the precision of the takedown.

jokes.text: Why are you using the urinal next to me? You leave a gap. I've already started. The little one gives me splash-back. Still, you leave a gap. We're not piss buddies.
jokes.setup: Richard approaches the urinal next to Jonah in the restroom.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's lecture on urinal etiquette — specifically the concept of 'piss buddies' — escalates from reasonable (leave a gap) to absurdist (the formal category of 'piss buddies' that they apparently don't qualify for).
jokes.explanation: 'Piss buddies' as a formal category of intimacy is the standout coinage here — it implies a whole taxonomy of bathroom relationships that Jonah has constructed. Richard's 'I've already started' as a counterargument to 'leave a gap' is perfectly logical within the scene's absurdist rules.

jokes.text: Damn, that's some kind of prostate control. Treasure it, my friend.
jokes.setup: Richard demonstrates impressive control of his bodily function during the urinal conversation.
jokes.punchline: Jonah — presumably older or having prostate concerns — regards Richard's bladder control with genuine awe and life-advice gravity.
jokes.explanation: 'Treasure it, my friend' as advice about prostate health delivered in a urinal scene is genuinely funny — the solemnity applied to bodily function is a good comedy register. Good economy.

jokes.text: So, you want to know if the president told me who she's firing, right? Yeah. It's me. You don't tell anyone until she's made an announcement. You got it? Yeah, not a word. You swear by everything you hold dear in your hand right now? I do. I swear on King Danny.
jokes.setup: Ben reveals to Jonah (in the urinal) that it's him being fired.
jokes.punchline: Jonah extracts a vow of secrecy from Richard by asking him to 'swear by everything you hold dear in your hand right now' — in a urinal scene; Richard solemnly swears 'on King Danny.'
jokes.explanation: A scene that builds to a perfect payoff. 'Swear by everything you hold dear in your hand right now' is a carefully constructed double entendre that works on both the physical and the ceremonial levels. 'I swear on King Danny' as Richard's solemn oath name for his genitalia is both absurd and entirely in character — Richard is so literal that he would name his genitalia and treat it as a formal oath object. One of the episode's strongest jokes for originality and structure. Earns 8.5.

jokes.text: Gary, front row for the story reading needs to be the children with the strongest bladders.
jokes.setup: Selina is organizing the Easter egg roll story reading.
jokes.punchline: Selina's primary criterion for front-row placement is bladder strength — purely to avoid the logistical problem of children needing to leave during the photo opportunity.
jokes.explanation: A perfect Selina line — children are set pieces in a photo opportunity, ranked by their photogenic reliability (no bathroom breaks). The bureaucratic logic applied to kids' biology is very sharp.

jokes.text: Why am I carrying eggs, huh? I'm up to my eyeballs in HIV. No, no, don't give them to me. There's a six-foot bunny out there with 30 spoons. You can't miss him.
jokes.setup: Mike is apparently being handed Easter eggs during the crisis.
jokes.punchline: Mike's complaint — 'I'm up to my eyeballs in HIV' — followed by Selina's completely practical instruction about the six-foot bunny — is a wonderful comic non-sequitur pivot.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'I'm up to my eyeballs in HIV' (a political crisis) with 'there's a six-foot bunny out there with 30 spoons' (Easter logistics) is quintessential Veep — the absurd institutional reality running alongside the political crisis. The six-foot bunny detail is nicely specific.

jokes.text: Well, thank you for your service, Greg. Very happy to have one of my Marines in costume. Actually, I'm really glad you're here. These kids terrify me. But I guess you've seen worse. You did two tours of Afghanistan? Yes, ma'am. I saw my best buddy die over there. Blown to pieces by an IED.
jokes.setup: Selina meets a man in a bunny costume, discovers he's a Marine.
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual 'you've seen worse' (than children) followed by the Marine's completely sincere and devastating response — 'I saw my best buddy die over there. Blown to pieces by an IED' — is a perfect Veep cringe escalation.
jokes.explanation: The joke is structural: Selina deploys a rhetorical comparison she expects to be vaguely positive (you've seen tough stuff, these kids are nothing), and the Marine answers with the most specific, devastating possible confirmation of 'worse things.' The pivot from Easter egg roll small talk to IED trauma is exactly the kind of tonal whiplash the show excels at. Earns 8.0 for structure and the darkness of the payoff.

jokes.text: Very happy to have one of my Marines in costume. / You're welcome, ma'am. / Actually, I'm really glad you're here. These kids terrify me. / But I guess you've seen worse. / You did two tours of Afghanistan? / Yes, ma'am. I saw my best buddy die over there. Blown to pieces by an IED.
jokes.setup: Selina discovers a Marine in the Easter Bunny costume and tries to bond with him.
jokes.punchline: Selina's attempt at casual military rapport ('These kids terrify me. But I guess you've seen worse') leads the Marine to describe his best friend being blown apart by an IED — a complete tonal mismatch with the Easter egg roll setting.
jokes.explanation: A perfect cringe escalation: Selina's attempt at military small talk ('you've seen worse') opens a door to genuine trauma that the Easter egg roll setting makes darkly absurd. The Marine's sincere, specific answer ('blown to pieces by an IED') against the backdrop of bunny costumes and children is classic Veep tonal whiplash.

jokes.text: I thought you resigned. I guess the president changed her mind. It's a fickle world, my friend, and you've just been fickled.
jokes.setup: Dan encounters Ben, who was supposed to have resigned but apparently hasn't.
jokes.punchline: 'Fickled' — using 'fickle' as a past-tense verb — is a neat neologism that captures exactly what happened to Dan in one invented word.
jokes.explanation: 'You've just been fickled' is an exceptional piece of language invention. The verb form of 'fickle' doesn't exist, but it's immediately comprehensible and perfectly precise. The setup ('it's a fickle world, my friend') makes the invented verb feel inevitable. This is the kind of wordplay that earns its 8.2 — it's genuinely novel and economically perfect.

jokes.text: No. No, this is not... This is not real. You're right, Dan. It's a dream. And me and Kent are about to turn into two horny cheerleaders and start making out.
jokes.setup: Dan protests that his firing is 'not real.'
jokes.punchline: Ben's escalation — confirming it IS a dream, then describing the dream's logical next phase (cheerleader makeout scene) — is a perfect deadpan absurdist rejection of Dan's denial.
jokes.explanation: Ben's deadpan commitment to the absurdist premise — 'you're right, it's a dream' — before extending it to its logical (dream) conclusion is a great piece of character comedy. The specificity of 'two horny cheerleaders' and 'start making out' is exactly the kind of absurdist precision that makes Veep's insults work. Earns 8.3.

jokes.text: So am I fired? Please, Ben, don't say that I'm fired. You're not fired. Oh, thank fuck for that. Because you've just resigned. It's a perfect fit.
jokes.setup: Dan begs not to be fired.
jokes.punchline: Ben fulfills the letter of Dan's request — 'you're not fired' — before landing 'because you've just resigned,' making the technical distinction the trap.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured misdirection joke. The 'you're not fired / because you've just resigned' structure is almost identical in elegance to the earlier 'the press doesn't need the culprit / the press needs a culprit' joke — both work by making an article or a phrasing do enormous work. Dan's genuine 'oh thank fuck for that' relief before the second hit lands is perfect timing. Earns 8.4 for structure.

jokes.text: I know about the targeting of bereaved families and the use of federal data. You listen to me, you little fucking turd's assistant. You don't threaten this administration because we will fucking destroy you. We'll skin you like a squirrel, clean you out like a dirty fucking chimney, and wear you like a glove puppet with my fingers sticking out of your dead fucking eyeballs.
jokes.setup: Dan tries to use his knowledge as leverage against being fired.
jokes.punchline: Ben's response is a three-part escalating threat of vivid violence: squirrel skinning → chimney cleaning → glove puppet with fingers through eyeballs. Each image is more specific and more disturbing than the last.
jokes.explanation: The episode's most explosive joke and a standout moment in the series. The three-part escalating threat is perfectly constructed: each image is drawn from a different domestic/rural register (hunting, home maintenance, children's entertainment), and they escalate in both specificity and horror. 'Wear you like a glove puppet with my fingers sticking out of your dead fucking eyeballs' is one of the most memorably violent threats in TV comedy. The escalation structure (skin → clean → wear) follows a logical sequence of animal processing. The 'little fucking turd's assistant' opener is a great piece of character positioning. Measured against the 9.5 anchor — this is in that territory for craft. The impact score of 9.5 reflects that this would stop a room cold before erupting.

jokes.text: I cannot endorse that message, but I do acknowledge it.
jokes.setup: Ben has just delivered an extreme threat to Dan.
jokes.punchline: Kent's precise bureaucratic response — 'I cannot endorse that message, but I do acknowledge it' — treats a murderous threat as if it were a press release requiring a measured response.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect joke. Kent's response is the ideal character-specific reaction to Ben's threat — applying legal/PR language ('endorse,' 'acknowledge') to a situation that has left those categories entirely. The distinction between 'endorse' and 'acknowledge' is precisely the kind of hairsplitting Kent would make. At 15 words, this is exceptional economy. The character integration is as high as any joke in the episode — this response could only come from Kent. Earns 9.1 craft — it's the episode's highest craft score because it takes the perfect word choices, makes the perfect structural distinction, and delivers in perfect character voice with perfect economy.

jokes.text: Yeah, you know what? I, uh... I think I'm gonna need a bigger title before I agree to resign.
jokes.setup: Dan is being read a children's fairy tale while simultaneously being forced to resign.
jokes.punchline: Dan's response to the situation — negotiating his title in the middle of being ousted, surrounded by Easter egg roll children — shows his unbreakable self-interest.
jokes.explanation: Dan's negotiation instinct in the middle of being destroyed is perfect character comedy. The absurdity of title-negotiating among children listening to Three Little Pigs is good contextual comedy.

jokes.text: How about Captain Loser? Or Big Chief Skidmark?
jokes.setup: Dan asks for a bigger title before he'll resign.
jokes.punchline: Ben offers two options — 'Captain Loser' and 'Big Chief Skidmark' — neither of which constitute titles.
jokes.explanation: 'Big Chief Skidmark' is the better of the two options — 'skidmark' as a presidential-adjacent title is absurd in exactly the right register. 'Captain Loser' is the weaker pairing but necessary to establish the two-option structure. Both work as character responses from Ben.

jokes.text: All right, how about Deputy Assistant to the President? That's funny.
jokes.setup: The title negotiation continues; Dan proposes 'Deputy Assistant to the President.'
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'That's funny' — delivered as a genuine laugh, not agreement — treats the title as a joke rather than a real option.
jokes.explanation: A good capper for the title negotiation sequence. Ben treating Dan's counteroffer as comedic entertainment rather than a real negotiation confirms the power dynamic. Functional closing beat.

jokes.text: Ben's not going. It's Dan. Mike's about to saw off the healthy leg.
jokes.setup: Amy discovers the wrong person is being announced as the departure.
jokes.punchline: 'Mike's about to saw off the healthy leg' — a medical metaphor implying Mike is making a terrible surgical error by announcing the wrong resignation — is vivid and specific.
jokes.explanation: The 'healthy leg' medical metaphor is particularly apt — amputating the wrong limb is both a medical catastrophe and a political one. The specificity of 'healthy leg' (implying they need to amputate a leg, just the right one) is dark and precise.

jokes.text: The person with ultimate responsibility is... is Dan Egan, Chief Counsel to the President?
jokes.setup: Mike is mid-announcement about who is resigning when he receives the updated name and has to pivot.
jokes.punchline: Mike's confused upward inflection on 'Dan Egan, Chief Counsel to the President?' — reading a name he clearly didn't expect — is a perfect cringe moment of live improvisation gone wrong.
jokes.explanation: The question mark at the end of 'Dan Egan, Chief Counsel to the President?' — Mike reading the new name live and not being sure if it's right — is perfectly Mike. The dramatic irony (we know it's correct, Mike doesn't know if it's correct) heightens the cringe. Earns 8.0 for structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Come over here, young man. It looks like you don't feel very good. Is that your son? He's gifted. There we go. Uh-oh. Oh. He did have a big breakfast.
jokes.setup: Selina is reading a children's story while the Dan resignation is announced, and simultaneously a child next to her appears to be vomiting.
