jokes.punchline: Selina's pivot from story-reading to managing a vomiting child — 'Uh-oh. Oh. He did have a big breakfast' — is a perfect Veep crisis-meets-mundanity beat, played entirely in public.
jokes.explanation: The comedic structure is the simultaneity — Dan's career is ending in the background while Selina deals with a vomiting child in the foreground. 'He did have a big breakfast' as a diplomatic explanation is exactly right. Good visual comedy moment.

jokes.text: Ew, you guys... Hey, man. Yeah, well, you know, enjoy it. I'm not here to gloat. I've been through this.
jokes.setup: Dan is watching Selina deal with the vomiting child while his own career is being announced as over.
jokes.punchline: Someone else apparently laughing at Dan ('Ew, you guys...') is met with Dan's 'I'm not here to gloat' — which means someone else is gloating, and Dan's nobility is immediately suspicious.
jokes.explanation: A transitional beat that works mainly as context-setting for Dan's exit. 'I've been through this' is a good character note — Dan has survived this before — but the beat is more functional than funny.

jokes.text: That photo of you with the kids will play great. Oh, it was so good. Yeah. It was like earth mother meets foster parent with a hint of girl next door.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to find a silver lining for Selina in the chaos of the Easter egg roll.
jokes.punchline: Gary's description — 'earth mother meets foster parent with a hint of girl next door' — is an absurdly focus-grouped description of a candid photo, delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Gary's description is a perfect piece of sycophantic marketing-speak. 'A hint of girl next door' in reference to the president dealing with a vomiting child is exactly the kind of brand-speak Gary applies to every Selina moment. The 'I need a scotch' response is the appropriate punchline.

jokes.text: Oh, God, I meant southeast gate.
jokes.setup: Gary told Dan to exit via the northwest gate; there's a large media presence there.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Gary sent Dan directly into the press pack — having told him it was clear — followed by Gary's horrified correction, implies Dan just walked straight into the press.
jokes.explanation: The timing of Gary's realization — after telling Dan to go north, when the media is at the northwest gate — is a perfectly constructed beat. The 'Oh, God' implies Gary knows exactly what he's done. Whether intentional or accidental is deliberately ambiguous, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Mr. Egan, do you feel ashamed of your actions?
jokes.setup: Dan exits the White House — directly into the press pack, due to Gary's confusion.
jokes.punchline: The first question is 'Do you feel ashamed of your actions?' — a maximally hostile opening that Dan has no prepared answer for.
jokes.explanation: The question itself isn't the joke — the context is. Dan being ambushed because of Gary's error, hit immediately with 'do you feel ashamed,' is the payoff of the gate sequence. Works better as a structure beat than a standalone joke.

jokes.text: I thought... When we discussed, I thought that it was Ben who would be going. Oh, right. Yeah, actually, Bill suggested Dan. He thought it would be a good idea to keep Ben to take the heat in case the other data shit hits the fan. Good thought, right? Yeah. Just doing my job. Me, too.
jokes.setup: Amy discovers that Dan — not Ben — was sacrificed.
jokes.punchline: The exchange 'Just doing my job. Me, too.' — between two people who just watched a colleague they manipulated get destroyed — is a perfectly chilling comedy beat.
jokes.explanation: The mirrored 'just doing my job' exchange is a perfect dark comedy capper to the Dan storyline. Two people who orchestrated someone's destruction trading professional mutual acknowledgment as if they'd coordinated a successful meeting. Earns 8.0 for the precision of the exchange and the character insight.

jokes.text: Ah. / He thought it would be a good idea to keep Ben to take the heat in case the other data shit hits the fan. / Good thought, right? / Yeah. / Just doing my job. / Me, too.
jokes.setup: Amy explains why Dan was chosen over Ben — it was Bill's suggestion, and Ben is being kept as a future scapegoat.
jokes.punchline: 'Just doing my job / Me, too' — the exchange between Amy and Selina about scapegoating people is delivered with the professional satisfaction of a job well done. The shared 'me too' of mutual scapegoating expertise is a perfect dark coda.
jokes.explanation: The professional satisfaction both women express about the scapegoating operation — 'just doing my job, me too' — is a darkly funny character beat. They're talking about destroying a colleague's career with the same tone as completing a performance review.

jokes.text: Stevie, it's Dan Egan. So, um... so it looks like I am a free agent again. Yeah, so give me a call. It's the same number as the last two messages. Hey, Jen, it's Dan. Either your phone is dead or I am. So obviously I hope it's the first one. Josh Stansfield. How are you? It's Dan Egan. So, look, I... And you've gone.
jokes.setup: Dan, having just been fired in front of the press, immediately starts cold-calling contacts to find his next job.
jokes.punchline: Each successive call goes worse: first a repeat message, then a voicemail where he jokes about being dead, then a call where someone hangs up mid-sentence. The escalating rejection is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant closing sequence that is both funny and devastating. 'It's the same number as the last two messages' implies Dan has already called twice; 'either your phone is dead or I am' is the show's best Dan line of the episode — gallows humor from a man who doesn't usually acknowledge his own mortality; 'And you've gone' is the perfect flat acceptance of being hung up on mid-pitch. The escalation from bad to worse to contemptuous is the show's thesis on Dan's relationship with power: he only exists in others' orbit. Earns 8.3 as a sustained comedic sequence.

tmdb_overview: Selina's team tries to find a scapegoat. Catherine wants to support an anti-bullying campaign.
insight: Eighty jokes in 35 minutes sustains Selina's chaos at peak velocity.
